config.title: GI Parasites - Clinical Presentation
config.subtitle: qbank generated by Claude for JeffMD GI week 3 Parasite LO: 'Correlate organisms commonly seen on stool ova and parasite evaluation with their microscopic appearance and clinical history' and parasites covered in lecture. Question format based on USMLE question guidelines (see guide)

questions.stem: A 28-year-old Peace Corps volunteer returns from Central America with 3 weeks of bloody diarrhea and right upper quadrant pain. He reports drinking unfiltered well water during his deployment. Stool O&P examination reveals 20 µm spherical cysts with up to 4 nuclei, each nucleus displaying a small, centrally placed karyosome and peripheral chromatin granules attached to the nuclear membrane. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
choices: Entamoeba histolytica
Giardia lamblia
Cryptosporidium parvum
Balantidium coli
Taenia solium
questions.explanation: The described cyst — spherical, 10–20 µm, up to 4 nuclei with a centrally placed karyosome and peripheral chromatin granules on the nuclear membrane — is pathognomonic for Entamoeba histolytica. G. lamblia cysts are oval with 4 nuclei but lack peripheral chromatin granules and are found in the small intestine, not associated with bloody colitis. C. parvum oocysts are much smaller (4–5 µm) and identified with acid-fast or fluorescent staining, not standard O&P morphology. The clinical picture of bloody dysentery with RUQ pain (amebic liver abscess) strongly reinforces E. histolytica.
keypoints: ★ E. histolytica cysts: spherical, 10–20 µm, 1–4 nuclei, centrally placed karyosome, peripheral chromatin granules on nuclear membrane
Trophozoites (20 µm) are found in liquid/bloody stool; cysts found in formed stool
RUQ pain + fever + liver abscess = extraintestinal amebiasis (hematogenous spread via portal vein)
Flask-shaped ulcers in submucosa are the histologic hallmark of intestinal amebiasis

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents to clinic with 2 weeks of foul-smelling, greasy diarrhea, upper abdominal cramping, and a 4 kg weight loss. She recently returned from a camping trip where she drank from a mountain stream. Stool O&P examination demonstrates oval cysts, 8–12 µm in size, containing 4 nuclei. Which morphological feature of the causative organism's trophozoite would be expected on duodenal aspirate?
choices: Two nuclei and four pairs of flagella with a large ventral sucker
Spherical body with a centrally placed karyosome and peripheral chromatin
Intracellular oocyst 4–5 µm attached to brush border microvilli
Hexacanth embryo within a thick-shelled egg
Barrel-shaped egg with bipolar plugs
questions.explanation: The cyst morphology (oval, 8–12 µm, 4 nuclei) and clinical syndrome of fatty malabsorption after drinking surface water are classic for Giardia lamblia. The trophozoite is stingray-shaped, 9–21 µm, with two nuclei and four pairs of flagella, and attaches to the intestinal brush border via a large ventral sucker. E. histolytica trophozoites have a single nucleus with a central karyosome. C. parvum is intracellular/extracytoplasmic and tiny (2.5 µm). Hexacanth embryos are found in cestode eggs. Barrel-shaped eggs with bipolar plugs are diagnostic of Trichuris trichiura.
keypoints: ★ Giardia trophozoite: stingray-shaped, 2 nuclei, 4 pairs of flagella, large ventral sucker
★ Giardia cyst: oval, 8–12 µm, 4 nuclei — found in formed stool
Mechanism of diarrhea: shortened brush-border microvilli → disaccharidase deficiency → fat/carbohydrate malabsorption
Giardia is a zoonosis (beavers are a classic reservoir); also transmitted person-to-person and via food

questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy with HIV is admitted for profuse watery diarrhea producing up to 15 liters per day. His CD4 count is 45 cells/µL. Stool evaluation using acid-fast staining reveals 4–5 µm oocysts. Electron microscopy would most likely show the causative organism in which location relative to intestinal epithelial cells?
choices: Intracellular but extracytoplasmic, beneath the cell membrane of microvilli
Free in the intestinal lumen, attached to brush border via a ventral sucker
Invading the submucosal layer and forming flask-shaped ulcers
Within the cytoplasm of enterocytes, causing direct cell lysis
In the lamina propria, engulfed by macrophages
questions.explanation: Cryptosporidium parvum is uniquely intracellular but extracytoplasmic — it resides just beneath the host cell membrane of microvilli rather than inside the cytoplasm. This unusual niche is a key distinguishing feature. The oocysts (4–5 µm) are identified with acid-fast or fluorescent staining. In immunocompetent patients, disease is self-limited (8–10 days); in AIDS patients with CD4 <100, voluminous watery diarrhea (up to 20 L/day) can persist until death. Giardia attaches via a ventral sucker but is extracellular. E. histolytica causes submucosal flask ulcers.
keypoints: ★ C. parvum: intracellular but EXTRACYTOPLASMIC — under cell membrane of microvilli
★ Acid-fast or fluorescent staining needed for oocyst identification (not standard O&P alone)
Oocysts (4–5 µm) are immediately infective when released in stool
In immunocompetent: self-limited 8–10 days; in AIDS: potentially fatal, voluminous watery diarrhea

questions.stem: A 24-year-old medical student volunteers at a clinic near the US-Mexico border. He sees a 9-year-old boy with new-onset seizures and no prior epilepsy history. CT scan shows multiple cystic lesions in the brain parenchyma and meninges. Stool examination of the boy's father, who shares the household, reveals eggs that are morphologically identical to those seen with a beef tapeworm infection. Which feature of the causative organism's scolex best distinguishes it from the beef tapeworm?
choices: Presence of 4 suckers plus a crown of hooks (rostellum)
Presence of 4 suckers and absence of hooks
Presence of grooves (bothria) instead of suckers
Scolex with a single large sucker and no hooks
Absence of a scolex due to larval stage invasion
questions.explanation: The clinical scenario describes neurocysticercosis, caused by Taenia solium larval cysts (cysticerci) in the CNS. The eggs of T. solium are identical to those of T. saginata on stool O&P, so scolex morphology is used to distinguish the two species. T. solium scolex has 4 suckers plus a double crown of hooks (armed/rostellate), while T. saginata scolex has 4 suckers but NO hooks (unarmed). Diphyllobothrium latum uses grooves (bothria) rather than suckers. Seizures, hydrocephalus, and stroke are the major manifestations of cysticercosis.
keypoints: ★ T. solium scolex: 4 suckers + crown of hooks (armed) — T. saginata: 4 suckers, NO hooks (unarmed)
★ Eggs of T. solium and T. saginata are IDENTICAL on O&P — proglottid morphology differentiates them
T. solium: 8–12 uterine branches per proglottid; T. saginata: 15–20 uterine branches
Neurocysticercosis = most common cause of adult-onset seizures in endemic regions; treat with albendazole + surgery

questions.stem: A 45-year-old Finnish man presents with progressive fatigue and pallor. Labs show a macrocytic anemia with hypersegmented neutrophils and low serum vitamin B12. He reports eating smoked salmon frequently. Stool O&P examination reveals an operculated (lid-like) egg approximately 70 µm in length. Which of the following best describes the life cycle stage through which this patient was infected?
choices: Plerocercoid larva encysted in the muscle of a freshwater fish
Hexacanth embryo ingested in undercooked beef
Cysticercus larva encysted in porcine skeletal muscle
Infective oocyst ingested in contaminated water
Filariform larva penetrating through intact skin
questions.explanation: Diphyllobothrium latum (fish tapeworm) causes vitamin B12 deficiency by competing with the host for intestinal B12 absorption, leading to pernicious-type macrocytic anemia — classically described in Finland. The operculated egg (~70 µm) in stool is diagnostic. Infection is acquired by eating raw or undercooked freshwater fish (salmon, trout, pike) containing the plerocercoid larval stage. T. saginata infection involves a hexacanth embryo in cattle. T. solium infection involves cysticercus larvae in pork. C. parvum uses oocysts. Hookworm uses filariform larvae penetrating skin.
keypoints: ★ D. latum diagnosis: operculated eggs in stool; scolex has bothria (grooves), NOT suckers
★ B12 deficiency mechanism: adult worm competes with host for vitamin B12 absorption in small intestine
Infection source: raw freshwater fish (salmon, trout, pike) containing plerocercoid larvae
70% of Finnish D. latum cases develop anemia; not seen in European or Japanese populations (dietary difference)

questions.stem: A mother brings her 5-year-old daughter to the pediatrician with a chief complaint of perianal itching, especially at night, causing sleep disturbances. The physician uses cellophane tape pressed to the perianal skin at dawn and examines it under the microscope. Which morphological feature of the eggs found would be most characteristic of this diagnosis?
choices: Eggs that are flattened on one side with an albuminous outer coating
Barrel-shaped eggs with prominent bipolar plugs
Operculated eggs approximately 70 µm in length
Thin-shelled eggs containing a morula-stage embryo
Spherical eggs with a thick outer wall and 6 hooks inside
questions.explanation: Enterobius vermicularis (pinworm) is diagnosed using the cellophane tape test applied to the perianal skin — ideally in the early morning before bathing. The eggs are characteristically flattened on one side (D-shaped) with an albuminous outer coating, distinguishing them from other helminth eggs. Trichuris trichiura eggs are barrel-shaped with bipolar plugs. Operculated eggs belong to Diphyllobothrium latum. Thin-shelled eggs with embryos are seen in hookworm. Hexacanth (6-hook) eggs characterize Taenia spp.
keypoints: ★ Pinworm eggs: D-shaped (flattened on one side), albuminous coating — diagnosed by cellophane tape test
★ Females migrate to perianal skin at night to lay eggs → pruritus ani, insomnia
Eggs are infective within hours; transmission: self-infection, environmental contamination, airborne
Treatment: mebendazole for entire family, repeat in 2 weeks to catch eggs that survived in environment

questions.stem: A 7-year-old boy from rural Guatemala is brought to clinic with chronic bloody diarrhea and rectal prolapse noted on exam. His CBC shows eosinophilia and iron-deficiency anemia. Stool O&P demonstrates characteristic eggs. Which of the following best describes the egg morphology of the causative organism?
choices: Barrel-shaped with prominent bipolar plugs, single-cell stage when laid
Flattened on one side with an albuminous coating
Operculated, approximately 70 µm
Spherical, 10–20 µm, with 1–4 nuclei and peripheral chromatin granules
Thin-shelled, containing 4- to 8-cell embryo
questions.explanation: Trichuris trichiura (whipworm) eggs are barrel-shaped (lemon-shaped) with distinctive bipolar plugs at each end and are laid in a single-cell stage. Heavy infections cause chronic bloody diarrhea, eosinophilia, iron-deficiency anemia from intestinal bleeding, and rectal prolapse in children due to straining — the described clinical picture. Pinworm eggs are D-shaped with an albuminous coat. D. latum eggs are operculated. E. histolytica cysts are spherical with peripheral chromatin. Hookworm eggs are thin-shelled with embryos.
keypoints: ★ T. trichiura eggs: barrel/lemon-shaped with bipolar plugs, laid in single-cell stage
Adult worm: thread-like anterior embedded in cecal mucosa, broad posterior ('whipworm' appearance)
Heavy infections: bloody diarrhea, rectal prolapse, iron-deficiency anemia, eosinophilia + Charcot-Leyden crystals in stool
Treatment: mebendazole, albendazole, or ivermectin (only symptomatic patients need treatment)

questions.stem: A 34-year-old man from sub-Saharan Africa presents with cough productive of sputum, transient pulmonary infiltrates on chest X-ray, and eosinophilia. He immigrated 10 weeks ago. Sputum examination reveals larvae and eosinophils. His symptoms then abate spontaneously, but 2 months later he returns with abdominal pain and vomiting. Stool O&P shows fertilized eggs. Which of the following best describes the migration route of the larval stage of the causative organism after egg ingestion?
choices: Duodenum → intestinal wall → mesenteric venules → liver → inferior vena cava → right heart → pulmonary vessels → alveoli → trachea → swallowed → small intestine
Skin → small blood vessels → right heart → lungs → epiglottis → small intestine
Oral ingestion → cyst excystation → colon lumen → submucosal invasion
Oral ingestion → sporozoite release → attachment to small intestinal microvilli → intracellular replication
Perianal skin → self-infection via hand-to-mouth → cecum and appendix
questions.explanation: Ascaris lumbricoides larvae hatch in the duodenum, penetrate the intestinal wall into mesenteric venules or lymphatics, travel through the liver and inferior vena cava (or thoracic duct) to the right heart, traverse the pulmonary vasculature, break into alveoli, and are coughed up and swallowed back to the small intestine. This migratory path causes Löffler's syndrome — transient eosinophilic pneumonitis with shifting pulmonary infiltrates and sputum eosinophilia. Hookworm follows a similar cardiopulmonary route but enters via skin penetration. E. histolytica invades colonic submucosa. C. parvum attaches to small intestinal microvilli.
keypoints: ★ Ascaris larvae: duodenum → intestinal wall → liver → right heart → lungs (Löffler's syndrome) → swallowed → intestine
★ Löffler's syndrome: transient eosinophilia + shifting pulmonary infiltrates + productive cough with larvae in sputum
Adult Ascaris: 30 cm, lives in small intestinal lumen; eggs survive in soil for up to 6 years
Acute febrile illness triggers worm migration → intestinal obstruction risk with heavy worm burdens (up to 2,000 worms)

questions.stem: A 19-year-old college student returns from a summer abroad in rural West Africa. He presents with iron-deficiency anemia, hypoalbuminemia, and peripheral edema. He walked barefoot frequently. Stool O&P shows thin-shelled eggs containing an embryo in the 4-cell stage. Which of the following best describes the mechanism by which this organism causes anemia?
choices: Adult worms attach to upper small intestinal mucosa and secrete anticoagulants, causing continuous hemorrhage
Larval migration through the bone marrow suppresses erythropoiesis
Adult worms compete with host for intestinal vitamin B12 absorption
Destruction of red blood cells within the intestinal lumen via lectin-mediated lysis
Cyst invasion of the spleen causing hypersplenism and hemolytic anemia
questions.explanation: Hookworm (Necator americanus / Ancylostoma duodenale) adults attach to the upper small intestinal mucosa and secrete anticoagulants to maintain blood flow as a nutritional source, causing iron-deficiency anemia through chronic hemorrhage. Hypoalbuminemia results from protein-losing enteropathy. Infection is acquired through skin penetration by larvae in soil (not ingestion), consistent with walking barefoot. Thin-shelled eggs with early-stage embryos in stool confirm the diagnosis. D. latum causes B12 deficiency via competition. E. histolytica uses lectin-mediated adherence but destroys tissue cells, not RBCs in lumen.
keypoints: ★ Hookworm anemia: anticoagulant secretion → continuous blood loss → iron-deficiency anemia + hypoalbuminemia
★ Hookworm eggs: thin-shelled, containing 4- or 8-cell embryo in fresh stool
Acquisition: filariform larvae penetrate intact skin (barefoot walking) → dermatitis at entry site
N. americanus has cutting plates; A. duodenale has teeth — both found in upper small intestine

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with rheumatoid arthritis on long-term prednisone presents to the emergency department with septic shock. He immigrated from Southeast Asia 30 years ago and never received screening for parasites. Blood cultures grow gram-negative rods. CT of the chest and abdomen shows larvae in the lungs, liver, and colon wall. Stool examination reveals larvae — not eggs — in the feces. Which feature of this organism's life cycle uniquely enables this clinical presentation?
choices: Autoinfection: first-stage larvae can develop into infective filariform larvae within the intestine and re-infect the host without exiting the body
Eggs hatch in the intestinal lumen and larvae penetrate into mesenteric venules before reaching the lungs
Oocysts undergo internal sporulation and release sporozoites that reinfect intestinal epithelium in immunosuppressed hosts
Cysts excystate in the colon and trophozoites reinvade the colonic mucosa in the same host
Proglottids disintegrate in the intestine and release hexacanth embryos that migrate hematogenously
questions.explanation: Strongyloides stercoralis is unique among nematodes in that it can undergo autoinfection: first-stage (rhabditiform) larvae develop into infective filariform larvae within the intestinal lumen or perianal skin, penetrate the bowel wall or perianal skin, and re-infect the same host without exiting. Under normal immune control, this maintains a chronic low-grade infection (infections lasting decades). Glucocorticoids (prednisone) suppress this immune control → unregulated hyperinfection → larvae disseminate carrying enteric gram-negative bacteria, causing sepsis. Larvae in stool (not eggs) distinguish Strongyloides from hookworm.
keypoints: ★ Strongyloides: LARVAE found in stool (not eggs) — key distinguishing diagnostic feature
★ Autoinfection: filariform larvae develop in intestine → penetrate bowel/perianal skin → chronic lifelong infection possible (up to 60 years)
Hyperinfection triggers: glucocorticoids, HTLV-1 infection, malnutrition, malignancy
Treatment: ivermectin (ALL infected individuals must be treated — unlike other nematodes)

questions.stem: A parasitology instructor presents four stool specimens from patients with diarrhea. Specimen A contains spherical cysts with a centrally placed karyosome and peripheral chromatin granules. Specimen B contains oval cysts with 4 nuclei and axostyles visible. Specimen C contains 4–5 µm oocysts that stain magenta on acid-fast preparation. Specimen D contains larvae in a fresh specimen. Which specimen is most consistent with a patient who has AIDS and is at risk for a fatal, disseminated infection?
choices: Specimen D
Specimen A
Specimen B
Specimen C
Specimens A and C equally
questions.explanation: Specimen D contains larvae, which is pathognomonic for Strongyloides stercoralis. In AIDS patients (and others who are immunosuppressed), S. stercoralis autoinfection becomes uncontrolled hyperinfection, with larvae disseminating to the lungs, liver, kidneys, heart, CNS, and other organs — carrying enteric bacteria and causing potentially fatal sepsis. C. parvum (Specimen C) causes severe diarrhea in AIDS patients but disseminated fatal disease with larvae is specific to Strongyloides. Specimen A = E. histolytica cysts; Specimen B = Giardia cysts.
keypoints: ★ Larvae in stool = Strongyloides stercoralis — no other intestinal nematode in this lecture sheds larvae in stool
★ In immunosuppressed hosts: S. stercoralis hyperinfection → disseminated, potentially fatal infection
S. stercoralis is the only intestinal nematode requiring treatment in ALL infected individuals regardless of symptoms
C. parvum is also dangerous in AIDS (voluminous watery diarrhea) but does not cause larval dissemination

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman presents with recurrent abdominal discomfort and notices worm-like segments actively crawling out of her anus. She eats beef tartare regularly at a local restaurant. Stool O&P and perianal examination are performed. Which morphological feature of the proglottid would best differentiate her infection from Taenia solium?
choices: 15–20 uterine branches per gravid proglottid
8–12 uterine branches per gravid proglottid
Uterine pore present for egg release without proglottid rupture
Scolex with a crown of hooks
Eggs containing non-hexacanth embryos
questions.explanation: Taenia saginata (beef tapeworm) gravid proglottids have 15–20 uterine branches, compared to 8–12 uterine branches in T. solium. Both produce identical eggs on O&P, so proglottid morphology is required to distinguish them. T. saginata proglottids actively migrate out of the anus — the primary complaint in this case. T. solium scolex has hooks; T. saginata does not. Diphyllobothrium latum releases eggs through a uterine pore rather than requiring proglottid rupture. All Taenia eggs contain hexacanth (6-hook) embryos.
keypoints: ★ T. saginata proglottid: 15–20 uterine branches; T. solium proglottid: 8–12 uterine branches
★ Eggs of T. saginata and T. solium are IDENTICAL — must examine proglottids or scolex to differentiate
T. saginata: 4 suckers, NO hooks; T. solium: 4 suckers + crown of hooks
T. saginata acquired via cysticercus in undercooked beef; adult worm only, no cysticercosis in humans

questions.stem: A 38-year-old male immigrant from Mexico presents with new-onset seizures and headache. MRI of the brain reveals multiple ring-enhancing cystic lesions. The patient admits he grew up in a household where pork was commonly consumed and hygiene was limited. His stool O&P is positive for eggs with hexacanth embryos. Which of the following mechanisms best explains how this patient's CNS findings developed?
choices: Human ingestion of T. solium eggs led to cysticercus larval development in CNS tissue after hematogenous migration
Human ingestion of T. solium cysticerci in undercooked pork led to adult worm intestinal infection and then larval CNS seeding
T. solium proglottids disintegrated in the small intestine and eggs were absorbed into the portal circulation
Autoinfection from perianal egg deposition caused transcutaneous larval penetration and CNS migration
Fish consumption led to plerocercoid larval dissemination into the cerebrospinal fluid
questions.explanation: Cysticercosis (larval CNS disease) results from humans ingesting T. solium EGGS — not cysticerci in pork. When humans ingest the eggs (via fecal-oral route from contaminated food, water, or autoinfection from an adult tapeworm carrier in the household), the hexacanth embryos hatch, penetrate the gut wall, enter the bloodstream, and migrate to skeletal muscle, subcutaneous tissue, or CNS where cysticerci (1 cm fluid-filled sacs) develop. Eating infected pork gives you the adult intestinal tapeworm — not cysticercosis. This distinction is critical for understanding pathogenesis.
keypoints: ★ Cysticercosis = ingestion of T. solium EGGS (fecal-oral); most common CNS location: meninges, ventricles, parenchyma
★ Intestinal tapeworm = ingestion of T. solium CYSTICERCI in undercooked pork — these are distinct diseases
Seizures, hydrocephalus, stroke = most common serious manifestations of neurocysticercosis
Serology unreliable for CNS infections; CT/MRI + CSF analysis used for cerebral cysticercosis diagnosis

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man presents to a travel clinic before a 6-month trip to rural India. He asks about the risk of parasitic infections. The physician explains that one intestinal parasite is notable because its cysts and oocysts survive chlorination of municipal drinking water and cannot be eliminated by standard water treatment. Which of the following organisms is correctly matched with this property AND its stool finding?
choices: Cryptosporidium parvum — 4–5 µm oocysts, acid-fast positive
Giardia lamblia — oval cysts, 8–12 µm, 4 nuclei, chlorine-resistant
Entamoeba histolytica — spherical cysts survive at 55°C and in chlorinated water
Strongyloides stercoralis — filariform larvae resist chlorination
Ascaris lumbricoides — fertilized eggs survive chlorination for years
questions.explanation: Cryptosporidium parvum oocysts (4–5 µm) are chlorine-resistant and can only be reliably eliminated by filtration if all water systems are well maintained. The oocysts remain viable for up to one year at 4°C and are immediately infective when shed in stool. While Giardia cysts are also not eliminated by routine chlorination (per lecture notes), C. parvum is the most clinically emphasized for this property and is identified by acid-fast or fluorescent staining — not standard O&P. E. histolytica cysts also survive chlorination (mentioned in lecture) but are identified by standard O&P morphology. The question asks for the organism correctly matched with BOTH properties.
keypoints: ★ C. parvum oocysts: chlorine-resistant, viable 1 year at 4°C; requires acid-fast or fluorescent staining
★ Giardia cysts: ALSO not eliminated by routine chlorination or filtration — important travel medicine point
E. histolytica cysts survive up to 55°C and in chlorinated water — but identified by standard O&P
C. parvum: only filtration (if well-maintained) reliably removes oocysts from water

questions.stem: A 12-year-old girl in rural Tennessee presents with abdominal discomfort and weight loss. She plays outside in soil near an outhouse. Stool O&P is performed and reveals eggs. Her CBC shows marked eosinophilia. If the eggs had hatched in the stool sample before examination (due to delayed processing), what would be found instead, and what diagnostic challenge would this create?
choices: Rhabditiform larvae that could be confused with Strongyloides stercoralis larvae normally found in stool
Filariform larvae that could be confused with Ascaris lumbricoides larvae in sputum
Trophozoites that could be confused with Entamoeba histolytica trophozoites
Cysticerci that could be confused with Taenia solium larval stages
Sporozoites that could be confused with Cryptosporidium sporozoites
questions.explanation: This case describes hookworm infection (N. americanus, endemic in rural southeastern US), acquired through skin penetration from soil-contaminated ground. Hookworm eggs are thin-shelled with embryos; if the stool sample is stored improperly or processed late, eggs hatch and release rhabditiform larvae. This creates a diagnostic challenge because S. stercoralis is also routinely diagnosed by finding larvae (not eggs) in feces, and hookworm larvae can be confused with Strongyloides larvae — a clinically important distinction given that S. stercoralis requires treatment in all infected individuals. Morphologic differentiation of larval stages is therefore essential.
keypoints: ★ Hookworm eggs hatch in improperly stored stool → larvae resemble S. stercoralis larvae — major diagnostic pitfall
★ S. stercoralis is diagnosed by larvae in stool (not eggs); hookworm normally by thin-shelled eggs with embryo
N. americanus: cutting plates in mouth; A. duodenale: teeth in mouth; both in upper small intestine
Hookworm infection: rural southern US, barefoot contact with contaminated soil; eosinophilia + iron-deficiency anemia

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with 3 weeks of watery, foul-smelling diarrhea and significant weight loss after returning from Peru. She reports no blood in the stool. Stool O&P reveals an organism with two nuclei and four pairs of flagella. Which of the following best describes the mechanism by which this organism causes diarrhea without invading the bowel wall?
choices: Trophozoites attach to brush-border microvilli via a ventral sucker, triggering apoptosis, tight junction disruption, and CD8+ lymphocyte activation leading to shortened microvilli and disaccharidase deficiency
Trophozoites invade the colonic submucosa and produce flask-shaped ulcers disrupting intestinal integrity
Intracellular oocysts disrupt enterocyte microvillar function by residing beneath the cell membrane
Adult worms embedded in the cecal mucosa trigger eosinophilic inflammation and bloody diarrhea
Cysts excystate in the colon and trophozoites secrete lectin-binding proteins that lyse mucosal cells
questions.explanation: Giardia lamblia trophozoites adhere to the small intestinal brush border via the ventral sucker without invading the bowel wall. The pathophysiology involves three mechanisms: (1) apoptosis of epithelial cells in contact with trophozoites, (2) disruption of epithelial tight junctions, and (3) CD8+ lymphocyte activation. Together these shorten brush-border microvilli, reduce disaccharidase and other enzyme activity, and cause malabsorption of fats and carbohydrates — explaining the foul-smelling, greasy, non-bloody diarrhea. E. histolytica invades the submucosa. C. parvum is intracellular/extracytoplasmic. T. trichiura threads into cecal mucosa. Lectin-mediated lysis is the mechanism of E. histolytica.
keypoints: ★ Giardia: non-invasive; watery/greasy malabsorptive diarrhea (fat + carbohydrate) — NO blood
★ Mechanism: ventral sucker adherence → apoptosis + tight junction disruption + CD8+ activation → microvilli shortening → disaccharidase deficiency
Upper abdominal cramping + foul-smelling greasy stool + weight loss = classic Giardia presentation
Treatment: metronidazole, tinidazole, or nitazoxanide

questions.stem: A 10-year-old boy from rural Brazil has a stool O&P that shows large fertilized eggs with a thick outer mammillated (bumpy) coat and inner thin shell, containing an unsegmented ovum. He has complaints of mild abdominal pain and visible worms in the stool. Peripheral blood eosinophilia is noted. His younger sister has had pulmonary symptoms with transient bilateral infiltrates on chest X-ray that resolved spontaneously. Which of the following correctly pairs the causative organism with the primary site of adult worm residence?
choices: Ascaris lumbricoides — lumen of the small intestine
Trichuris trichiura — lumen of the small intestine
Necator americanus — cecum and appendix
Enterobius vermicularis — upper small intestine
Strongyloides stercoralis — lumen of the small intestine
questions.explanation: The egg description — large, thick outer mammillated coat, inner thin shell, unsegmented ovum — is classic for Ascaris lumbricoides. Adult Ascaris (up to 30 cm) reside in the lumen of the small intestine, wedging themselves in position. The sister's pulmonary symptoms with transient bilateral infiltrates are Löffler's syndrome from larval migration. Trichuris adults embed their thread-like anterior end in the cecal/appendiceal mucosa. N. americanus adults attach to the upper small intestinal mucosa (not cecum). Pinworm adults attach to the cecum and appendix. Strongyloides parthenogenetic females reside in the mucosal epithelium of the upper small intestine.
keypoints: ★ Ascaris lumbricoides: largest intestinal nematode (30 cm); eggs — thick mammillated coat, single-cell embryo
Adult Ascaris: small intestinal lumen; wedge themselves in position by motility
Löffler's syndrome: larval lung migration → transient eosinophilic pneumonitis with shifting infiltrates
Eggs survive in moist warm soil for up to 6 years; 1 billion infected worldwide

questions.stem: A physician is reviewing four stool O&P results from patients in an infectious disease clinic. Which of the following pairings of organism and diagnostic finding is INCORRECT?
choices: Strongyloides stercoralis — eggs with hexacanth embryos found in fresh stool
Entamoeba histolytica — trophozoites found in liquid stool, cysts in formed stool
Diphyllobothrium latum — operculated eggs found in stool
Giardia lamblia — oval 4-nuclei cysts found in formed stool, trophozoites in diarrheal stool
Trichuris trichiura — barrel-shaped eggs with bipolar plugs found in stool
questions.explanation: Strongyloides stercoralis is uniquely diagnosed by finding LARVAE (not eggs) in fresh stool — it is the only intestinal nematode in these lectures where larvae are routinely shed in feces. Eggs of S. stercoralis hatch within the intestinal epithelium, and larvae are what exit into the intestinal lumen and then stool. All other pairings are correct: E. histolytica trophozoites appear in liquid stool and cysts in formed stool; D. latum produces operculated eggs; Giardia cysts are oval with 4 nuclei in formed stool; T. trichiura produces barrel-shaped eggs with bipolar plugs.
keypoints: ★ Strongyloides: LARVAE in stool (not eggs) — only intestinal nematode in lecture with this characteristic
★ E. histolytica: trophozoites in liquid/bloody stool; cysts in formed stool — always check both types
D. latum: operculated eggs (lid-like opening); scolex distinguished by bothria (grooves)
Diagnostic summary: O&P method and stool consistency determine which forms are found for each organism

questions.stem: A 30-year-old immunocompetent man with no travel history presents with 9 days of explosive watery diarrhea after attending a day care center with his 3-year-old child. Stool O&P is negative for cysts or ova on standard wet mount. Modified acid-fast staining of stool reveals numerous 4–5 µm oval oocysts staining magenta. He is reassured that he will recover without specific treatment. Which of the following features of this organism best explains why routine O&P wet mount was negative?
choices: The oocysts are too small to be reliably visualized on standard wet mount and require specific acid-fast or fluorescent staining for identification
The organism has no cyst or oocyst stage and is only found as a trophozoite in liquid stool
The organism is intracytoplasmic and not shed into the intestinal lumen
The organism is sexually reproducing inside macrophages and is not found in stool
Standard wet mounts are insufficient because the organism requires serological testing for diagnosis
questions.explanation: Cryptosporidium parvum oocysts (4–5 µm) are extremely small — at the lower limit of light microscope resolution for O&P wet mounts — and do not have the characteristic morphological features of larger cysts that are identifiable on standard preparation. Modified acid-fast or direct fluorescent antibody (DFA) staining is required for reliable diagnosis, as oocysts stain distinctively magenta/red against a blue-green background. This patient is immunocompetent; C. parvum infection in healthy individuals is self-limited (8–10 days), though patients remain infectious for >2 weeks. The organism does have an oocyst stage — it is not exclusively a trophozoite.
keypoints: ★ C. parvum standard O&P wet mount is often NEGATIVE — acid-fast or fluorescent staining required
★ Oocysts (4–5 µm): immediately infective when shed; person-to-person is primary transmission route
Immunocompetent: self-limited 8–10 days; remains infectious for 2+ weeks after symptom resolution
Day care centers are a recognized epidemiological setting for C. parvum outbreaks

questions.stem: A parasitology practical exam presents five microscopic images. Image 1 shows a large worm segment with a central rosette-shaped uterus filled with eggs and 15–20 lateral branches. Image 2 shows a scolex with two groove-like structures (bothria). Image 3 shows stool with larvae (not eggs). Image 4 shows small D-shaped eggs with one flattened side. Image 5 shows 20 µm spherical cysts with 4 nuclei, peripheral chromatin granules, and a central karyosome. Which image–organism pairing is correct?
choices: Image 1 = Taenia saginata proglottid; Image 2 = Diphyllobothrium latum scolex; Image 3 = Strongyloides stercoralis; Image 4 = Enterobius vermicularis; Image 5 = Entamoeba histolytica
Image 1 = Taenia solium proglottid; Image 2 = Taenia saginata scolex; Image 3 = hookworm; Image 4 = Trichuris trichiura; Image 5 = Giardia lamblia
Image 1 = Diphyllobothrium latum proglottid; Image 2 = Taenia solium scolex; Image 3 = Ascaris lumbricoides; Image 4 = Cryptosporidium; Image 5 = Entamoeba histolytica
Image 1 = Taenia saginata proglottid; Image 2 = Taenia solium scolex; Image 3 = Strongyloides stercoralis; Image 4 = Enterobius vermicularis; Image 5 = Giardia lamblia
Image 1 = Taenia solium proglottid; Image 2 = Diphyllobothrium latum scolex; Image 3 = Strongyloides stercoralis; Image 4 = Enterobius vermicularis; Image 5 = Entamoeba histolytica
questions.explanation: Image 1 (proglottid with 15–20 uterine branches) = T. saginata (T. solium has 8–12 branches). Image 2 (bothria/grooves on scolex) = D. latum — the only cestode in these lectures using groove-like bothria rather than suckers. Image 3 (larvae in stool) = S. stercoralis — the only organism that sheds larvae rather than eggs in stool. Image 4 (D-shaped egg, flattened on one side) = E. vermicularis (pinworm) — characteristic egg shape diagnosed by cellophane tape test. Image 5 (spherical cyst with 4 nuclei, central karyosome, peripheral chromatin granules) = E. histolytica — not Giardia, which lacks peripheral chromatin granules.
keypoints: ★ T. saginata proglottid: 15–20 uterine branches; T. solium: 8–12 branches
★ D. latum scolex: bothria (grooves), NOT suckers; Taenia spp. scolex: suckers (±hooks)
★ Larvae in stool = Strongyloides; D-shaped eggs = pinworm (cellophane tape test); spherical cyst with peripheral chromatin = E. histolytica
★ Giardia cysts: oval, 4 nuclei, axostyles visible — NO peripheral chromatin granules (key E. histolytica differentiator)
